title: Common Mistakes to Avoid When Hiring Movers
excerpt: Avoid written-scope gaps, skipped surveys, weak packing, ignored building rules, too many bosses, valuables on truck, unmonitored doors, blank signatures, price-only picks, tight elevators, and stacked schedules.
content:
Common mistakes to avoid when hiring movers

Most move-day disasters are predictable. These mistakes show up across Addis Ababa weekly—avoid them and you avoid most conflict and damage.

Mistake 1: No written scope

Verbal prices without line items invite bait-and-switch patterns. Insist on written estimates covering labor, truck, fuel, materials, stairs, and cancellation.

Mistake 2: Skipping the survey

Large moves guessed over the phone underestimate volume. Video or in-person walkthroughs sharpen crew count and truck size.

Mistake 3: Weak owner packing

Overfilled large boxes, single-tape bottoms, and unlabeled fragile cartons slow crews and increase breakage.

Mistake 4: Ignoring building rules

Elevator reservations, ID lists, and loading hours exist for a reason. Gate rejections burn your paid hours.

Mistake 5: Three people directing the crew

Conflicting instructions from relatives confuse pacing. One household decision-maker speaks to the lead.

Mistake 6: Valuables on the truck

Cash, jewelry, passports, and original deeds belong with a person—not in a carton.

Mistake 7: Propped doors with no monitor

Open doors during load are theft windows. Rotate a watcher or ask the lead for door duty.

Mistake 8: Signing blank or unread paperwork

Read completion documents. Note visible issues before signing.

Mistake 9: Choosing purely on lowest price

Outliers twenty percent below the pack often omit stairs, fuel, or materials. Compare normalized bids.

Mistake 10: Zero buffer on elevator bookings

Finish early better than fight the next reservation mid-sofa. Book extra minutes.

Mistake 11: Last-minute schedule chaos

Same-day key handoffs and overlapping cleaner appointments stack risk. Build slack.

Recovery if you already made one

Pause, photograph, call the supervisor, clarify scope in email. Documentation fixes more than shouting.

Mistake 12: Ignoring insurance caps

Assume nothing about full replacement value—read the clause before load.

Mistake 13: Cash-only with no receipt

Traceable payment protects you if disputes or reimbursements arise.

Conclusion

Hiring movers is a project. Written scope, honest access, strong boxes, single-voice communication, and security basics eliminate most mistakes before they cost money.
author: Active Movers Team
